text: The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and scissors to make a longitudinal incision through the myometrium, carefully cutting until the capsule of the submucous myoma is exposed.
Upon locating the myoma, the surgeon applies traction to the myoma to achieve hemostasis and begins removing the connective tissue between the myoma and the surrounding healthy myometrium.

The surgeon extends the longitudinal incision slightly to facilitate removal of the myoma. Using forceps, traction and counter-traction are applied to carefully extract the myoma from its bed after removing the capsula.

The surgeon carefully places forceps below the submucous myoma to avoid damaging the endometrium, applying traction to extract the myoma from its bed. The assistant uses additional forceps to aid in the removal process.

The surgeon applies energy directly to the myoma, avoiding the healthy myometrium. Small bleeders surrounding the myoma are removed, and the myoma is completely extracted from its bed. The surgeon inspects for larger bleeders, performing small coagulations, with hemostasis achieved through stitches.
The surgeon uses a 14mm needle with 0 Vicryl to perform interrupted sutures, placing each stitch 2.5 centimeters outside the scar on both sides of the incision.
The surgeon invaginates healthy tissue into the myoma bed, ensuring strong cicatrization and reducing the risk of intrauterine hematoma. The endometrium is visibly placed inside the myoma bed.

The surgeon locks the knot using the intracorporeal knotting technique, securing the suture around the five-centimeter myoma. The second knot is placed one or two centimeters lateral to the edge of the scar, using the same needle and technique, before removing the needle and repeating the movement on the opposite side.

The surgeon uses minimal bipolar energy to preserve healthy tissue for optimal cicatrization. Intracorporeal knotting is performed to invaginate healthy tissue into the myoma bed, promoting strong healing.

The surgeon rinses the pelvis to remove blood and decrease the risk of post-operative adhesions. The final knot is tied superiorly using the same intracorporeal knotting technique, ensuring minimal trauma to preserve fertility.

The surgeon ties the last knot using the intracorporeal knotting technique, ensuring the stitch does not penetrate the cornea. The small myoma is visible and will be removed following the completion of knotting.
The surgeon demonstrates a perfect coaptation between the two edges of the uterine scar, with no visible bleeding from the scar site.

The surgeon inserts a morcellator through the left lateral trocar to begin morcellating the myoma. The pelvis is visibly clean with no blood present. A fascia closer is used to close the trocar site, which is larger than twelve millimeters, to reduce the risk of accidental bowel hernia.
